umbrellas.label: Presence of Others and Individual Behavior
umbrellas.description: How being observed or working alongside others changes individual performance, effort, and self-regulation — covering facilitation, loafing, deindividuation, and the bystander effect.

intro.label: How Others Shape Individual Behavior
intro.description: The mere presence of other people can dramatically alter how we perform and behave. Sometimes others boost performance (social facilitation), sometimes they reduce effort (social loafing), sometimes they erode individual identity (deindividuation), and sometimes their sheer number paradoxically reduces helping (bystander effect). These effects show that individual behavior is never truly independent of the social context.
examples: diffusion of responsibility in a crowd
loss of self-awareness in a group

in_depth.label: Social facilitation
in_depth.description: Identify social facilitation: the presence of others improves performance on well-learned or simple tasks but impairs performance on novel or complex tasks. This focuses on the performance-change due to an audience or co-actors, not on reduced effort when working in a group (social loafing, a separate keyword).
examples: cyclists ride faster with others present

in_depth.label: Social loafing
in_depth.description: Recognize social loafing: individuals exert less effort on a collective task when their personal contribution cannot be identified than when working alone. This focuses on the diffusion of effort in a group, not on the diffusion of responsibility in an emergency (bystander effect, a separate keyword).
examples: less effort in a group tug-of-war
contribution drops as group size grows

in_depth.label: Deindividuation
in_depth.description: Define deindividuation: being in a crowd or wearing a uniform reduces self-awareness and personal accountability, leading to impulsive or antisocial behavior. This focuses on loss of individual identity in a group context, not on reduced helping behavior in emergencies (bystander effect, a separate keyword).
examples: anonymity in a crowd → increased aggression

in_depth.label: Bystander effect
in_depth.description: Explain the bystander effect: the more people who witness an emergency, the less likely any individual is to intervene, driven by diffusion of responsibility and pluralistic ignorance. This focuses on helping in emergencies with multiple observers, not on the general reduction of effort in group work (social loafing, a separate keyword).
examples: diffusion of responsibility and pluralistic ignorance

in_depth.label: Diffusion of responsibility
in_depth.description: Distinguish diffusion of responsibility: each member of a group feels less personally obligated to act because others share the burden, reducing the likelihood of prosocial behavior. This focuses on the psychological mechanism underlying the bystander effect, distinguishable from pluralistic ignorance (the separate misreading of others' inaction as a signal that no emergency exists).
examples: key mechanism in the bystander effect

in_depth.label: Pluralistic ignorance
in_depth.description: Identify pluralistic ignorance: each group member privately doubts a situation is an emergency but, seeing others remain calm, incorrectly concludes that it must not be serious. This focuses on the misinterpretation of others' calm behavior as a social cue, not on the diffusion of felt obligation (diffusion of responsibility, a separate keyword).
examples: no one reacts → 'must not be serious'
group reads smoke alarm as false alarm

umbrellas.label: Social Control, Peer Pressure, Conformity, and Obedience
umbrellas.description: The mechanisms by which society and other individuals pressure people to align their beliefs or actions with group expectations — ranging from informal peer norms to direct authority commands.

intro.label: Social Influence: Control, Conformity, and Obedience
intro.description: Humans are powerfully shaped by the people and institutions around them. Social control refers to societal mechanisms that enforce normative behavior; peer pressure represents informal interpersonal influence; conformity is the tendency to match group behavior even without direct requests; and obedience involves complying with explicit authority commands. Classic studies by Asch (conformity) and Milgram (obedience) are foundational benchmarks for understanding how far social influence extends.

in_depth.label: Social control
in_depth.description: Define social control: the formal and informal mechanisms societies use to encourage conformity to norms and deter deviance, including laws, norms, rewards, and punishments. This focuses on the macro-level societal processes that regulate behavior, not on individual-level interpersonal pressure (peer pressure, a separate keyword).
examples: gossip and shaming as informal control

in_depth.label: Peer pressure
in_depth.description: Recognize peer pressure: the informal influence exerted by people of equal social standing that nudges individuals toward group-consistent behaviors, beliefs, or attitudes. This focuses on interpersonal influence among peers, not on compliance with authority figures (obedience, a separate keyword).
examples: adolescent smoking to fit in with friends
dress codes driven by peer expectations

in_depth.label: Conformity
in_depth.description: Explain conformity: adjusting one's behavior or beliefs to match the perceived group standard, even without direct pressure to do so, driven by normative influence (wanting acceptance) or informational influence (using the group as a source of information). This focuses on spontaneous alignment with the group, not on compliance with a direct order from an authority (obedience, a separate keyword).

in_depth.label: Asch conformity study
in_depth.description: Recall the Asch line-judgment paradigm: confederates gave obviously wrong answers, and naive participants conformed roughly 37% of the time, demonstrating that social pressure can override clear perceptual evidence. This focuses on the classic empirical demonstration of conformity, not on obedience to authority (Milgram study, a separate keyword).

in_depth.label: Normative vs. informational social influence
in_depth.description: Distinguish normative influence (conforming to gain approval or avoid rejection) from informational influence (conforming because you genuinely use the group as a source of evidence). This focuses on the two motivational roots of conformity, not on the obedience-to-authority dynamic (a separate keyword).

in_depth.label: Obedience to authority
in_depth.description: Define obedience: complying with a direct command from a perceived authority figure, even when the command conflicts with personal values. This focuses on explicit authority-driven compliance, not on spontaneous conformity to group norms (conformity, a separate keyword).

in_depth.label: Milgram obedience study
in_depth.description: Recall the Milgram shock experiment: ~65% of participants delivered apparently dangerous shocks to a confederate when ordered by an authority figure, showing the power of situational authority over individual moral judgment. This focuses on the canonical empirical study of obedience, not on the Asch conformity paradigm (a separate keyword).

in_depth.label: Factors that affect obedience
in_depth.description: Identify the situational variables that increase or decrease obedience: proximity to the victim, legitimacy and proximity of authority, presence of dissenting peers, and institutional prestige. This focuses on what modulates the degree of obedience, not on the original Milgram finding itself (a separate keyword).
examples: victim in same room → less obedience

umbrellas.label: Group Decision-Making
umbrellas.description: How group deliberation and composition distort decisions — specifically the polarization of initial positions and the suppression of dissent in cohesive groups.

intro.label: Group Decision-Making: Polarization and Groupthink
intro.description: When individuals deliberate together, group processes can push decisions in predictable and often problematic directions. Group polarization drives group decisions toward more extreme versions of members' initial leanings; groupthink occurs when the desire for harmony silences critical evaluation. Understanding these two phenomena explains why committees, juries, and organizations often make worse decisions than individuals acting alone.
examples: jury deliberation shifts to extreme verdict

in_depth.label: Group polarization
in_depth.description: Explain group polarization: group discussion intensifies members' initial average opinion, producing a more extreme collective position than members held before deliberating. This focuses on the shift-toward-extremity pattern in group deliberation, not on the suppression of dissent in cohesive groups (groupthink, a separate keyword).
examples: initial pro-X majority → strongly pro-X outcome

in_depth.label: Groupthink
in_depth.description: Identify groupthink: a mode of thinking in which the desire for cohesion and unanimity in a tight-knit group overrides realistic appraisal of alternatives, leading to flawed decisions. Symptoms include illusions of invulnerability, collective rationalization, and self-censorship. This focuses on cohesion-driven suppression of dissent, not on the directional intensification of pre-existing views (group polarization, a separate keyword).

in_depth.label: Symptoms and prevention of groupthink
in_depth.description: Recognize the classic symptoms of groupthink (illusion of invulnerability, mind-guards, out-group stereotyping, self-censorship) and the structural remedies (devil's advocate, leader withholding opinion, outside experts). This focuses on identifying and preventing groupthink, not on defining its core mechanism (a separate keyword).

umbrellas.label: Social Norms
umbrellas.description: The shared rules and expectations that define acceptable behavior in society — from informal customs to codified laws — and the sanctions that enforce them.

intro.label: Social Norms, Sanctions, and the Architecture of Conformity
intro.description: Social norms are the unwritten (and written) rules that tell us what is expected in a given social situation. They vary in strength — from casual folkways to moral mores to absolute taboos — and are enforced through sanctions ranging from a disapproving look to criminal prosecution. Anomie describes what happens when norms break down. Together, these concepts explain how order and predictability are maintained in social life.
examples: violation of a taboo triggers strong sanction
Durkheim's anomie in rapid social change

in_depth.label: Social norms as shared expectations
in_depth.description: Define social norms as the shared expectations — explicit or implicit — that guide behavior within a group or society, distinguishing prescriptive norms (what you should do) from proscriptive norms (what you must not do). This focuses on the concept and typology of norms, not on the specific strength categories of folkways, mores, and taboos (covered in a separate keyword).
examples: holding the door open as a prescriptive norm
norms vary by culture and context

in_depth.label: Sanctions
in_depth.description: Distinguish positive sanctions (rewards for norm compliance) from negative sanctions (punishments for violations), and formal sanctions (official legal penalties) from informal sanctions (social disapproval, gossip, ostracism). This focuses on how norms are enforced, not on the norms themselves (a separate keyword).
examples: praise for helping a stranger (positive informal)
social exclusion for norm violation (negative informal)

in_depth.label: Folkways, mores, and taboos
in_depth.description: Classify norms by their moral weight: folkways are mild conventions whose violation brings mild disapproval (e.g., table manners); mores are stronger norms tied to moral values whose violation brings serious sanctions; taboos are the most extreme prohibitions (e.g., incest, cannibalism) considered utterly wrong in virtually all cultures. This focuses on the three-level strength hierarchy of norms, not on how sanctions are applied (a separate keyword).
examples: chewing with mouth open = folkway violation

in_depth.label: Anomie
in_depth.description: Define anomie (Durkheim): a condition of normlessness in which social norms are weak, absent, or contradictory, often occurring during rapid social change, leaving individuals without clear guidance and increasing deviant behavior and suicide rates. This focuses on the breakdown of norms at the societal level, not on individual motivation to deviate (covered by strain theory, a separate keyword).
examples: Durkheim's anomic suicide during economic upheaval

umbrellas.label: Deviance
umbrellas.description: Behavior that violates social norms and the major sociological theories explaining why deviance occurs — differential association, labeling, and strain theory.

intro.label: Deviance: Definition and Sociological Perspectives
intro.description: Deviance refers to any behavior, belief, or condition that departs from social norms and invites social disapproval. Sociologists explain deviance through several competing theoretical lenses: differential association emphasizes learned pro-deviance definitions; labeling theory focuses on how society's reactions create deviant identities; and strain theory argues that blocked access to legitimate goals pushes individuals toward deviant means. Each theory illuminates a different causal pathway.
examples: crime as the most formal type of deviance
societal reaction as a cause of deviance
why do conforming people occasionally deviate?

in_depth.label: Deviance — definition and types
in_depth.description: Define deviance as behavior, beliefs, or characteristics that violate the norms of a group or society and elicit negative social reactions; distinguish formal deviance (crime) from informal deviance (social disapproval without legal consequences). This focuses on the basic concept and classification, not on the theories explaining its causes (separate keywords).
examples: deviance is relative to culture and context

in_depth.label: Differential association theory
in_depth.description: Explain Sutherland's differential association theory: people learn deviant behavior through close associations with others who hold favorable definitions of deviance — more exposure to pro-deviant attitudes than anti-deviant attitudes increases the likelihood of deviance. This focuses on the social-learning mechanism, not on blocked legitimate pathways to goals (strain theory, a separate keyword).
examples: frequency and intensity of associations matter
Sutherland: crime is learned, not inherited

in_depth.label: Labeling theory
in_depth.description: Explain labeling theory (Becker): deviance is not an intrinsic quality of an act but a consequence of society applying the deviant label; being labeled deviant can become a self-fulfilling master status. This focuses on the societal reaction as constitutive of deviance, not on the social transmission of deviant norms (differential association, a separate keyword).
examples: societal reaction creates the deviant identity

in_depth.label: Primary vs. secondary deviance
in_depth.description: Distinguish primary deviance (initial norm-violating acts, before a deviant identity is adopted) from secondary deviance (deviant behavior that follows and is shaped by the internalization of a deviant label). This focuses on Lemert's two-stage process within labeling theory, not on the labeling mechanism itself (a separate keyword).

in_depth.label: Strain theory (Merton)
in_depth.description: Explain Merton's strain theory: deviance results from a disjunction between culturally valued goals (e.g., financial success) and the legitimate means to achieve them; individuals blocked from legitimate means may adapt through deviant routes (innovation, ritualism, retreatism, or rebellion). This focuses on structural-goal/means mismatch as the cause of deviance, not on the social-learning mechanism (differential association, a separate keyword).
examples: innovation: pursues goals by illegal means
retreatism: abandons both goals and means

in_depth.label: Merton's modes of adaptation
in_depth.description: Classify Merton's five adaptations to strain: conformity (accepts goals + means), innovation (accepts goals, rejects means), ritualism (rejects goals, keeps means), retreatism (rejects both), and rebellion (replaces both). This focuses on mapping each adaptation type, not on the overall strain framework (a separate keyword).
examples: innovation: drug dealer pursues wealth illegally

umbrellas.label: Collective Behavior
umbrellas.description: Spontaneous or emergent group behaviors — fads, mass hysteria, and riots — that arise outside of established social institutions and often spread rapidly through populations.

intro.label: Collective Behavior: Fads, Hysteria, and Riots
intro.description: Collective behavior refers to relatively spontaneous, unstructured action by large numbers of people in response to a shared event or sentiment. Unlike institutionalized behavior, it emerges from a crowd dynamic rather than formal rules. MCAT-relevant examples include fads (fleeting mass enthusiasms), mass hysteria (collective anxiety or belief), and riots (explosive collective aggression). Understanding these phenomena requires considering how social conditions and contagion processes amplify individual responses.
examples: Salem witch trials as mass hysteria
urban riot following a triggering incident

in_depth.label: Fads and crazes
in_depth.description: Define fads as temporary, widespread enthusiasms for a behavior, style, or product that spread quickly and fade; distinguish them from fashions (longer-lasting cycles) and from more intense collective behaviors. This focuses on the short-lived mass-enthusiasm phenomenon, not on more intense collective behaviors like riots or mass hysteria (separate keywords).

in_depth.label: Mass hysteria
in_depth.description: Define mass hysteria (mass psychogenic illness): a collective episode in which anxiety, fear, or physical symptoms spread through a social network in the absence of a genuine organic cause, sustained by suggestion and shared belief. This focuses on the collective spread of psychosomatic response, not on the crowd aggression of riots (a separate keyword).
examples: school-wide fainting episodes without medical cause

in_depth.label: Riots
in_depth.description: Characterize riots as collective episodes of violence or property destruction involving large numbers of people, often triggered by a precipitating event against a backdrop of social tension, and propagated through crowd contagion. This focuses on collective violence and its social preconditions, not on non-violent collective panics (mass hysteria, a separate keyword).

umbrellas.label: Socialization
umbrellas.description: The lifelong process by which individuals learn the values, norms, and behaviors of their society through interaction with key social institutions — family, peers, schools, mass media, and workplace.

intro.label: Socialization and Its Agents
intro.description: Socialization is the process through which people internalize the culture of their society — its values, norms, language, and roles — shaping a social self. It begins at birth (primary socialization within the family) and continues throughout life (secondary socialization in schools, peer groups, workplaces, and mass media). Each agent of socialization contributes differently to the internalization of social expectations, and their relative influence shifts across the life course.
examples: family as the first socializing agent

in_depth.label: Socialization — definition and types
in_depth.description: Define socialization as the process by which individuals learn the norms, values, language, and behaviors of their culture; distinguish primary socialization (early childhood, family) from secondary socialization (later agents including school, peers, workplace). This focuses on the concept and stages, not on the specific agents (separate keywords).
examples: child learns language and gender roles from family
school teaches societal expectations and discipline

in_depth.label: Family as agent of socialization
in_depth.description: Identify the family as the primary socialization agent: it conveys core values, language, cultural identity, and initial norms about gender, class, and authority during the most formative developmental period. This focuses on the family's unique first-mover role, not on later agents like peers or mass media (separate keywords).
examples: family transmits religious and ethnic identity

in_depth.label: Peer group as agent of socialization
in_depth.description: Explain the peer group as a socialization agent: peers become increasingly influential in adolescence, shaping values, attitudes, identity, and behavioral norms outside parental control, and providing a reference group for social comparison. This focuses on the peer group's role distinct from the family's (a separate keyword).
examples: teen friend groups set fashion and language norms
peer acceptance shapes identity in adolescence
peer group as adolescent reference group

in_depth.label: School as agent of socialization
in_depth.description: Describe schools as formal socialization agents that transmit academic knowledge alongside the hidden curriculum — implicit lessons about authority, punctuality, competition, and civic norms. This focuses on schools' dual role in formal and hidden-curriculum socialization, not on peer influence within the school (a separate keyword).
examples: hidden curriculum: learning to follow rules
school socializes children into civic roles
tracking and ability grouping reproduce social hierarchy

in_depth.label: Mass media as agent of socialization
in_depth.description: Identify mass media as a pervasive secondary socialization agent that shapes norms, values, and self-concept by broadcasting cultural scripts and representations to large audiences simultaneously. This focuses on media's broad top-down influence, distinguishable from peer (interpersonal) or family (intimate) socialization agents (separate keywords).
examples: news frames social reality for audiences
social media reinforces identity and group norms

in_depth.label: Workplace as agent of socialization
in_depth.description: Recognize the workplace as a secondary socialization agent that teaches occupational norms, professional identity, hierarchical expectations, and organizational culture through anticipatory socialization and on-the-job experience. This focuses on adult occupational socialization, not on childhood or adolescent agents (separate keywords).
examples: new employee learns unwritten office norms
anticipatory socialization before entering a field

in_depth.label: Resocialization and total institutions
in_depth.description: Define resocialization: the process of discarding old norms and roles to adopt new ones, often occurring in total institutions (Goffman) such as prisons, military boot camps, and psychiatric hospitals that control nearly all aspects of daily life. This focuses on radical norm-replacement in controlled environments, not on ordinary secondary socialization (separate keyword).
examples: military boot camp strips civilian identity
prison resocializes inmates to institutional norms
Goffman: total institution controls time and space
